data.title: EPC: Feminine Care divestiture, margin recovery, and international growth drive FY26 outlook
data.description:
Announced Feminine Care divestiture to focus on core categories, with FY25 marked by strong international growth but margin and earnings pressure from tariffs, FX, and inventory adjustments. FY26 guidance targets flat to modest sales growth, margin recovery, and debt reduction, with most earnings weighted to the second half.

data.title: Tetra Tech Shares Rise as 4Q Rev Gains on Continued Demand
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

Tetra Tech shares rose after the consultancy saw a quarter of strong demand for its water-consulting services.

The stock was up 15%, trading at $37.17. Shares have fallen 6.7% year to date.

The company late Wednesday reported fourth-quarter net revenue of $1.16 billion, compared with $1.14 billion one year earlier. Analysts polled by FactSet were expecting $1.06 billion.

Earnings were 48 cents a share, compared with 35 cents a share one year earlier.

Adjusted earnings were 45 cents a share. Analysts expected 40 cents a share.

"These all-time high results were driven by the continued strong demand for our differentiated high-end consulting services in resilient water management and digital water automation," Tetra Tech Chief Executive Officer Dan Batrack said.

The Pasadena, Calif., company said it anticipates net revenue for the current quarter between $950 million and $1 billion. It forecasted earnings of 30 cents to 33 cents a share for the quarter.

Tetra Tech also projected full-year net revenue between $4.05 billion and $4.25 billion, representing year-over-year growth between 5% and 11%. Earnings for the full year are expected at $1.40 to $1.55 a share.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: Tencent Music Ent Is Maintained at Buy by Benchmark
Here's where more than 1,000 Starbucks baristas are said to be striking, as union drive nears 4-year anniversary
data.description:
By Bill Peters and Tomi Kilgore

Union says baristas at 65 stores across more than 40 cities are striking on 'Red Cup Day.' Labor expert says companies have felt emboldened to avoid bargaining.

A labor strike by more than 1,000 Starbucks baristas in 42 cities has begun, timed with the coffeehouse chain's annual "Red Cup Day" giveaway.

The federal government may have just resumed operations, but many Americans could be facing another shutdown on Thursday - at their local Starbucks.

Timed to coincide with the coffee chain's (SBUX) annual "Red Cup Day," on which customers can receive a free reusable holiday-themed cup, the Starbucks Workers United union said more than 1,000 baristas have launched an "open-ended" labor strike to demand better pay and improved staffing.

The union said the strike would begin with 65 stores across more than 42 cities. The work action, it said, could expand to the more than 550 stores in which baristas are represented if a labor deal isn't reached.

More than 12,000 Starbucks workers have unionized since late 2021, when baristas first voted to organize at a store in Buffalo, N.Y. But since then, negotiations over a contract have stumbled, and the union has accused the company of scores of labor-law violations, bad-faith bargaining and trying to suppress organization.

"Starbucks' refusal to settle a fair union contract and end union busting is forcing us to take drastic action," Dachi Spoltore, a barista in Pittsburgh, Pa., said in a statement on Thursday.

After the union voted last week to authorize the strike, Starbucks' chief partner officer, Sara Kelly, said unionized employees represented less than 4% of coffeehouse workers, and that more than 10,000 stores and nearly 7,000 licensed locations would remain open. Starbucks also questioned the numbers of workers the union said were participating in the strike and of the cities affected.

Jaci Anderson, director of global communications at Starbucks, said in a statement that Starbucks was "disappointed" that Workers United called for a strike instead of coming back to the bargaining table.

"Less than 1% of our coffeehouses are experiencing any level of disruption and the vast majority of our 240,000 partners came to work ready to serve customers and celebrate Reuseable Red Cup Day," she said.

Starbucks stock was down 0.7% in recent afternoon trading, after closing the previous session at a 10-week high. The shares have lost 5% so far this year, while the S&P 500 index SPX has rallied 14.5%.

The strike comes amid Starbucks' efforts to revive sales growth, as it deals with stiffer competition from smaller coffee chains and customers struggling with higher costs of living. Some customers have complained that Starbucks stores lack the warmer atmospheres of years past, and were too focused on driving traffic and churning out orders.

Under Chief Executive Brian Niccol, who took the job last year, Starbucks has embarked on a plan to make store atmospheres more welcoming, with things like condiment bars, comfier seats and ceramic mugs. The chain has also said it has taken steps intended to untangle the ordering process, boost staff hours, and make service feel more personal.

The union has claimed that the plan isn't working, saying customers and baristas aren't happy. Still, in Starbucks' latest quarterly report, the company reported year-over-year growth in comparable-store sales for the first time in seven quarters.

Management credited extra staffing, improved service and things like new protein drinks for those results. But it held off on providing a more detailed outlook on its business until next year. The company has reported contractions in consolidated operating margin, a measure of how profitable sales are, for seven straight quarters, due to higher coffee costs and tariffs, but also because of higher labor costs.

Thursday's strikes received some national attention. Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, in a post on X, said she expressed solidarity with the striking workers.

"Starbucks employees have long fought against consistently unpredictable schedules, short staffing, low pay, and unfair labor practices," she said.

Starbucks and the union committed to negotiating a framework for contracts last year. Talks followed, and both sides reached an array of tentative agreements on how to improve the workplace. But talks later stalled, and the union in April this year rejected a proposal from Starbucks that they said "failed to improve wages or benefits in the first year of the contract" and didn't address understaffing.

After the union voted to authorize a strike last week, Starbucks said it offered "the best job in retail." The company said it was investing $500 million to boost staffing, and rolling out technology to make working easier.

Kelly, in that announcement, said: "Our commitment to bargaining hasn't changed." She added: "We believe we can move quickly to a reasonable deal."

Still, the unionization drive, and Starbucks' efforts to stop it over the past few years, have challenged the company's image as a progressive-minded employer. The union, in materials sent out in conjunction with the strikes, said that the average barista receives less than 20 hours a week, below the cut-off for benefits, and said that understaffing was "rampant, leading to longer wait times as customer orders stream in."

Still, lax labor laws can make it easy for employers to drag out the negotiation process with unionized employees or otherwise stamp out unionization efforts.

Kate Bronfenbrenner, a lecturer at Cornell University's School of Industrial and Labor Relations, said in a statement on Wednesday corporations were likely emboldened by a "dysfunctional" National Labor Relations Board and an "anti-union administration."

"Once again, Starbucks workers are striking and engaging customer support to get Starbucks back to the table," she said. "They may also need to again mount a large campaign with investors and other stakeholders to convince Starbucks that reaching a first contract is in the company's best interest."

Here are the cities in which the strike has begun, according to the union:

  • Anaheim, Calif.
  • Long Beach, Calif.
  • San Diego, Calif.
  • Santa Clarita, Calif.
  • Santa Cruz, Calif.
  • Scotts Valley, Calif.
  • Seal Beach, Calif.
  • Soquel, Calif.
  • Colorado Springs, Colo.
  • Lafayette, Colo.
  • Alpharetta, Ga.
  • Roswell, Ga.
  • Des Plaines, Ill.
  • Evanston, Ill.
  • Geneva, Ill.
  • Chanhassen, Minn.
  • Minneapolis, Minn.
  • St. Louis, Mo.
  • Brooklyn, N.Y.
  • New York, N.Y.
  • Columbus, Ohio
  • Lewis Center, Ohio
  • Reynoldsburg, Ohio
  • Upper Arlington, Ohio
  • Worthington, Ohio
  • Beaverton, Ore.
  • Damascus, Ore.
  • Eugene, Ore.
  • Gresham, Ore.
  • Portland, Ore.
  • Dickson City, Pa.
  • Lancaster, Pa.
  • Philadelphia, Pa.
  • Pittsburgh, Pa.
  • Austin, Texas
  • Dallas, Texas
  • Denton, Texas
  • Farmers Branch, Texas
  • Mechanicsville, Va.
  • Richmond, Va.
  • Redmond, Wash.
  • Seattle, Wash.

-Bill Peters -Tomi Kilgore

ESALQ Poultry Analysis
data.description: poultry analysis POULTRY/CEPEA: Exports are the 2nd highest in history Cepea, 13 - As expected by Cepea, the volume of chicken meat exported in October was the second highest in history - the record was verified in March 2023. It is worth noting that this good performance was verified despite the absence of China in sales. The country returned buying from Brazil on November 7, endingthe suspension imposed by different countries after the bird flu case registered in Brazil in May this year. Exports to China were suspended for six months and, now, the total volume of shipments is expected to increase again. Data from Secex indicate that Brazil exported 501.3 thousand tons of chicken meat (in natura and industrialized products) in October, 3.95% more than the amount sold in September/25 and 8.2% above that verified in October/24. Thus, the volume sold in the first 10 months of 2025 is practically the same as that observed in the same period last year. This scenario indicates that Brazilian exports are likely to hit a record again. Although the volume shipped increased, the income was below that expected by the sector. Data from Secex indicate that the amount was at BRL 4.6 billion in October, 1.2% more than in September, but dropping 8.4% against Oct/24. In the first five producing days of November, the daily average of international sales is at 27 thousand tons, a record for Secex series (since 1997), 25.4% above that presented in October/25 and 17.6% more than in Nov/24. For the Portuguese version please refer to or click on <ESQ/POULT/ANALYS>

data.title: 21Shares launches crypto index ETFs under SEC’s Act 40
data.description:
Asset manager 21Shares has launched two cryptocurrency index exchange-traded funds (ETFs) regulated under the Investment Company Act of 1940, a structure that could boost investor confidence by subjecting the products to the same disclosure and governance rules that apply to traditional US investment funds.

The new products — the 21Shares FTSE Crypto 10 Index ETF (TTOP) and the 21Shares FTSE Crypto 10 ex-BTC Index ETF (TXBC) — were announced on Thursday. Both offer broad exposure to digital assets by tracking FTSE Russell cryptocurrency indexes and holding a basket of the top crypto assets by market capitalization, rather than investing in a single token.

Federico Brokate, 21Shares’ global head of business development, said that index funds have enabled investors to gain diversified exposure to traditional assets, particularly stocks. “The same principle applies to crypto investing,” he said.

21Shares has been highly active in the crypto exchange-traded product market and was recently acquired by FalconX for an undisclosed amount. The company will continue to operate independently under the FalconX umbrella.

Related: SEC says proof-of-work mining does not constitute securities dealing

The significance of the Investment Act of 1940

The Investment Company Act of 1940 is the regulatory framework that governs US mutual funds and most conventional ETFs, imposing requirements around custody and investor protections. 

This contrasts with the Securities Act of 1933, which is used for grantor trust structures that hold physical commodities — the model regulators have mainly applied to US spot crypto products to date.

As Cointelegraph reported, the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) has already approved a crypto ETP under the ’33 Act — the Rex-Osprey Doge ETF, which launched in September.

Until now, the SEC has mainly approved spot Bitcoin (BTC) and Ether (ETH) products under the ’33 Act, rather than as fully regulated, investment-company ETFs under the ’40 Act. 

Demand for crypto ETFs has been high since spot Bitcoin funds debuted in early 2024. BlackRock has led the pack: Its IBIT Bitcoin ETF amassed roughly $70 billion in assets under management within its first year and a half on the market.

data.title: RLI Corp Declares Special and Regular Dividends
data.description:
On November 13, 2025, RLI Corp announced a special cash dividend of $2.00 per share and a regular quarterly dividend of $0.16 per share.

Key Highlights:

  • Special dividend totals approximately $184 million.
  • Dividends payable on December 19, 2025.
  • Shareholders must be on record by November 28, 2025.
  • Reflects RLI's strong balance sheet and strategic confidence.
  • Over $1.6 billion returned to shareholders in the past decade.

Original SEC Filing: RLI CORP [ RLI ] - 8-K - Nov. 13, 2025

data.title: KURA: FDA approved KOMZIFTI for relapsed/refractory NPM1-mutated AML, offering a new oral therapy
data.description:
FDA approved KOMZIFTI, the first once-daily oral menin inhibitor for adults with relapsed or refractory NPM1-mutated AML, based on the KOMET-001 trial. The drug showed deep, durable responses and a manageable safety profile, addressing a critical unmet need.

Original document: Kura Oncology, Inc. [KURA] Press release — Nov. 13 2025

data.title: Fitch Affirms Lloyds Banking Group's IDR at 'A+'; Outlook Stable
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-London-13 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has affirmed Lloyds Banking Group plc's (LBG) Long-Term Issuer Default Rating (IDR) at 'A+' with a Stable Outlook and its Viability Rating (VR) at 'a+'.

Fitch has also affirmed the Long-Term IDRs of LBG's ring-fenced bank subsidiaries, Lloyds Bank plc (LB), HBOS plc, Bank of Scotland Plc (BOS) and Lloyds Bank GmbH (LGmbH), and its non-ring-fenced bank subsidiaries, Lloyds Bank Corporate Markets plc (LBCM) and Lloyds Bank Corporate Markets Wertpapierhandelsbank GmbH (LBCMW), at 'AA-' with a Stable Outlook. A full list of rating actions is below.

Key Rating Drivers

Leading UK Bank: LBG's ratings reflect its solid, diversified business profile in the UK, supported by its leading retail and business banking franchises and insurance operations. They also reflect consistently solid profitability, as well as strong capitalisation and funding and liquidity.

Group VR: LBG acts as the holding company for the group, and its VR is equalised with that of its core banks, LB and BOS, reflecting high integration and low double leverage. The subsidiaries' IDRs are one notch higher than their VRs as external senior creditors are protected by resolution funds down-streamed from LBG.

Conservative Risk Profile: LBG's risk profile is underpinned by the high share of low-risk mortgage lending (67% of loans at end-3Q25) with low loan-to-value ratios. Conservative underwriting standards mitigate risks from unsecured consumer loans (6% of gross loans) and commercial lending (19%). Our assessment also incorporates LBG's strong and resilient performance through economic cycles, underpinned by robust risk controls.

Strong Asset Quality: We expect LBG's asset quality to remain strong over the next two years, with the impaired loans ratio (end-3Q25: 1.8%) remaining below 2%. We expect asset quality to be supported by falling interest rates. Loan impairment charges (LICs) should remain easily absorbable, given the largely secured loan portfolio.

Consistently Solid Profitability: LBG's profitability has been consistently strong and compares well with UK and European peers'. We expect operating profit/risk-weighted assets (RWAs; 9M25: 3.2%) to strengthen to about 3.5% in 2026 as structural hedge income more than offsets margin pressure in mortgage lending and deposits, while cost growth and LICs are contained. Revenue diversification and strategic investments into non-interest income- generating businesses should also support earnings.

Strong Capitalisation: We expect LBG's capitalisation to remain strong and commensurate with its rating despite its target to reduce the common equity Tier 1 (CET1) ratio (end-3Q25: 13.8%) to 13% by end-2026. Our assessment is supported by LBG's solid capital generation and prudent risk management. Profitability is solid and able to absorb potential additional charges linked to historical motor finance commission arrangements.

Sound Funding and Liquidity: LBG's funding and liquidity profile is sound, supported by a large and granular deposit base and leading franchise. We expect the loans/deposits ratio (end-3Q25: 97%) to remain broadly stable and liquidity to remain strong. Funding stability is underpinned by proven access to diversified wholesale funding and contingent Bank of England facilities.

LBG's Short-Term IDR of 'F1' is the lower of two options mapping to the 'A+' Long-Term IDR, as our assessment of the group's funding and liquidity profile does not warrant a higher rating. The Short-Term IDRs of LBG's subsidiaries are 'F1+', the only option for 'AA-' Long-Term IDRs.

Rating Sensitivities Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

LBG's ratings could come under pressure from an increase in the four-year average impaired loans ratio to above 3% without a clear path to reduction, and a sustained deterioration in operating profit to below 2.5% of RWAs leading to the CET1 ratio falling below 13% on a sustained basis.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

An upgrade of LBG's ratings is unlikely, given the group's high concentration in the UK market. An upgrade would require a substantial strengthening of the group's business profile, alongside a continued record of solid operating profitability, plus strong asset quality and capitalisation.

OTHER DEBT AND ISSUER RATINGS: KEY RATING DRIVERS

The long-term senior unsecured debt and Derivative Counterparty Rating (DCR) of LBG and of its subsidiaries, where applicable, are in line with their respective Long-Term IDRs.

Tier 2 debt issued by LBG and HBOS is rated two notches below LBG's VR. This is the baseline notching under our criteria, reflecting high loss severity for this type of debt.

The ratings of legacy upper Tier 2 subordinated debt issued by LBG, LB, HBOS and BOS are notched down three times: twice for loss severity and once for incremental non-performance risk, from their respective VRs (LBG's VR for HBOS).

Additional Tier 1 (AT1) and legacy Tier 1 and preferred stock issued by LBG are four notches below the anchor VR, two each for loss severity and incremental non-performance risk. Our assessment is based on the group operating with a CET1 ratio comfortably above the maximum distributable amount thresholds and we expect this to continue.

GOVERNMENT SUPPORT RATINGS (GSR)

The GSRs of 'no support' for LBG, LB and BOS reflect Fitch's view that senior creditors cannot rely on extraordinary support from the UK authorities if these issuers become non-viable, due to UK legislation and regulations that provide a framework requiring senior creditors to participate in losses after a failure.

OTHER DEBT AND ISSUER RATINGS: RATING SENSITIVITIES

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

The senior unsecured debt and DCRs of the entities, where applicable would be downgraded if their Long-Term IDRs were downgraded.

The ratings on Tier 2 debt, AT1, legacy Tier 1 debt and preferred stock, where relevant, would be downgraded if the respective VRs were downgraded.

AT1 and other discretionary Tier 1 instruments are also sensitive to Fitch changing its assessment of the probability of their non-performance relative to the risk captured in LBG's VR and could be downgraded if the headroom to minimum distributable amount thresholds drops below 100bp.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

The long-term senior unsecured debt ratings and DCRs would be upgraded if the Long-Term IDRs of the respective entities were upgraded.

The Tier 2 debt, AT1, legacy Tier 1 debt and preferred stock ratings would be upgraded if the respective VRs were upgraded.

Any upgrade of the GSRs of LBG, LB and BOS would be contingent on a positive change in the sovereign's propensity to support domestic banks, which Fitch considers highly unlikely in light of the resolution framework in place.

SUBSIDIARIES & AFFILIATES: KEY RATING DRIVERS

The 'a+' Shareholder Support Ratings (SSRs) of HBOS, LBCM and LBCMW reflect a very high probability of shareholder support, given their strategic roles in the wider group, high level of ownership by LBG and reputational risks for LBG from a default. LGmbH's 'aa-' SSR reflects its direct and strategic ownership by LB.

The 'AA-' Long-Term IDRs of LB and BOS are notched up once from their VRs to reflect the presence of a large buffer of qualifying junior debt and senior holding company debt down-streamed in a subordinated manner, underpinned by minimum requirement for own fund and eligible liabilities (MREL) regulations. We also incorporate this benefit into the IDRs of HBOS, LBCM, LBCMW and LGmbH. These entities have not been assigned VRs as Fitch believes that these entities cannot be assessed meaningfully on a standalone basis. Their IDRs reflect a very high probability of shareholder support from LBG.

SUBSIDIARIES AND AFFILIATES: RATING SENSITIVITIES

The subsidiaries' IDRs that benefit from an uplift due to resolution funds could be downgraded if we no longer expected the group's resolution planning and requirements to afford the subsidiaries' senior creditors protection after a group failure. The SSRs and IDRs of HBOS, LBCM, LGmbH and LBCMW are also sensitive to a reduction in their parents' ability or propensity to support the entities.

The subsidiaries' SSRs and IDRs would be upgraded if LBG's IDRs were upgraded.

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. ESG Considerations

The highest level of ESG credit relevance is a score of '3', unless otherwise disclosed in this section. A score of '3' means ESG issues are credit-neutral or have only a minimal credit impact on the entity, either due to their nature or the way in which they are being managed by the entity. Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores are not inputs in the rating process; they are an observation on the relevance and materiality of ESG factors in the rating decision. For more information on Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores, visithttps://www.fitchratings.com/topics/esg/products#esg-relevance-scores.

data.title: Fitch Affirms Nationwide Building Society at 'A'; Stable Outlook
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-London-13 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has affirmed Nationwide Building Society's Long-Term Issuer Default Rating (IDR) at 'A' with a Stable Outlook and Viability Rating (VR) at 'a'. In addition, Fitch has affirmed the Long-Term IDRs of Virgin Money UK PLC (VMUK), a fully owned subsidiary of Nationwide, and its main operating bank, Clydesdale Bank plc (CB), at 'A' with Stable Outlooks.

Fitch has upgraded the VRs of VMUK and CB to 'a', to a group VR in line with Nationwide's VR, from 'bbb+', reflecting our view that the VMUK subgroup has the same failure risk as the wider Nationwide group, given high integration and the expected Part VII transfer of CB into Nationwide in 2026.

Key Rating Drivers

Low Risk, Undiversified Business Model: Nationwide's ratings reflect a fairly stable, low-risk business model focused largely on residential mortgage loans, funded by retail savings and current accounts. Asset quality is healthy, funding, liquidity and capitalisation are solid, and profitability is sound but subject to competitive pressure and integration costs in the short term. Nationwide's acquisition of VMUK has added scale, but its business model and earnings remain less diversified than larger UK bank peers'.

Acquisition Consolidates Market Position: Nationwide's acquisition of VMUK in 2024 consolidated the group as the UK's second-largest mortgage lender and largest buy-to-let mortgage lender, and materially increased its market shares. The acquisition has enlarged Nationwide's current account franchise and broadened its product offering, including to business clients. Nevertheless, the group remains highly concentrated in UK mortgages and reliant on net interest income.

Reduced Integration Risks: Nationwide's risk appetite is conservative and underpinned by a focus on low-risk residential mortgage lending (91% of lending at FYE25 (year-end March 2025)). We believe credit risks have increased only slightly with the combination of VMUK, given the latter's higher-risk credit card and business lending. Nevertheless, these risks are mitigated by the small size of the portfolios and Nationwide's prudent risk controls. We believe integration risks have reduced compared with a year ago, due to Nationwide's good progress in integrating VMUK.

Strong Asset Quality: Nationwide's impaired loans ratio increased to 1% at FYE25 from 0.8% at end-September 2024, due largely to the acquisition of VMUK, but the underlying performance of the loan book has been resilient. We forecast the impaired loans ratio will slightly weaken, mainly in credit card and business lending, but to remain below 1.5%. The group's cost of risk will be structurally slightly higher, given higher-risk exposures, although this could be offset by higher margins from these businesses.

Integration Costs Weighing on Profitability: Nationwide benefited from a large one-off gain of GBP2.3 billion from the VMUK acquisition, but underlying profitability weakened, primarily reflecting acquisition-related costs. Integration and restructuring costs are likely to weigh on Nationwide's profitability as material synergies and cost savings are unlikely in the next two years. We therefore expect operating profit/risk-weighted assets (RWAs; four-year average: 3.2%) to remain weaker at about 2% in FY26-FY27, before gradually improving toward 2.5% in FY27 with realised synergies, a larger scale and higher margins. Earnings diversification is narrower than at larger peers.

Weaker CET1 Ratio: Nationwide's common equity Tier 1 (CET1) ratio fell to 18.8% at end-1QFY26 (FYE24: 27.1%), due primarily to the net effects of the acquisition of VMUK, and it will likely only improve to about 20% in 2027, due to weaker profitability and asset growth. However, capitalisation remains strong for its low-risk business with buffers well above regulatory minimum requirements. Fitch expects Nationwide's UK leverage ratio (end-1QFY26: 5.2%) to remain above 5%.

Solid Funding and Liquidity: Nationwide's funding and liquidity profile reflects its stable and granular deposit base and strong liquidity. The acquisition of VMUK has strengthened its deposit offering by increasing current account balances and providing funding diversification through business banking deposits. Funding stability is underpinned by proven access to diversified wholesale funding and contingent Bank of England facilities.

Group VR: The IDRs of VMUK and CB are equalised with those of Nationwide and reflect the group VR, reflecting the high integration of the subgroup into Nationwide. Integration has increased since the acquisition including through the combination of boards, the alignment of governance structures and financial integration following the completion of Nationwide's consent exercise for most of VMUK's minimum requirement for own funds and eligible liabilities (MREL) debt.

Rating Sensitivities Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

Nationwide's ratings would come under pressure if integration risks materialised and weighed materially and for a prolonged period on the group's strategic execution and financial performance.

Ratings would also come under pressure if risk appetite materially increased and resulted in greater asset-quality deterioration than our expectations, for example, if we expected the four-year average impaired loans ratio to increase materially above 2% on a sustained basis. This would have to be combined with a structural deterioration in operating profitability - such as the operating profit/RWAs declining consistently below 2% on a four-year average basis.

Effects of the acquisition, rapid asset growth and weaker-than-expected profitability that resulted in the CET1 ratio staying materially below 20% and the UK leverage ratio falling materially below 5%, without credible plans to swiftly restore them, would be negative for the ratings.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

An upgrade of Nationwide's ratings is unlikely in the near term due to its undiversified business model compared with at higher rated peers. Over the long term, and with a successful integration of VMUK, a significant strengthening of Nationwide's business profile (e.g. materially stronger revenue diversification), material improvements to operating profitability (to over 3% of RWAs), combined with healthy capital and liquidity buffers, could lead to positive rating action.

OTHER DEBT AND ISSUER RATINGS: KEY RATING DRIVERS

Nationwide

The Short-Term IDR of 'F1' maps to the lower of two options for a Long-Term IDR of 'A' because we assess the society's funding and liquidity at 'a', which does not warrant a higher Short-Term IDR.

Nationwide's long-term senior preferred (SP) debt and deposit ratings and Derivative Counterparty Rating (DCR) are one notch above the Long-Term IDR. This is because we expect Nationwide to continue to meet its MREL with senior non-preferred (SNP) and qualifying junior debt (largely additional Tier 1 (AT1) and Tier 2 debt buffers). These buffers protect its preferred creditors, depositors and derivative counterparties.

The short-term SP debt and deposit ratings of 'F1' are the lower of two options mapping to the long-term SP debt and deposit ratings of 'A+' because the 'a' funding and liquidity score does not allow for higher short-term ratings.

Nationwide's subordinated debt and hybrid securities are notched down from the VR, reflecting their incremental non-performance risk and assumptions around loss severity. The lower Tier 2 subordinated debt is rated two notches below the VR for loss severity. The permanent interest-bearing securities and AT1 debt are rated four notches below the VR, reflecting two notches for their deep subordination and two notches for incremental non-performance risk. Nationwide's AT1 securities are converted into core capital deferred shares on breach of a capital trigger. Their coupon payment is fully discretionary.

VMUK and CB

We have affirmed VMUK's and CB's Short-Term IDRs at 'F1', in line with Nationwide's Short-Term IDR. CB's 'F1' short-term deposit rating is in line with its Short-Term IDR.

We have affirmed VMUK's senior unsecured debt rating in line with the Long-Term IDR.

VMUK's AT1 debt rating of 'BBB-' is notched down three times from the Long-Term IDR and is capped by the rating of the equivalent instrument issued by Nationwide.

Fitch has affirmed CB's DCR, one notch above its Long-Term IDR, reflecting the protection for creditors arising from the requirement for Nationwide to raise and downstream a large buffer of SNP and junior debt to the VMUK subgroup. For the same reason, CB's long-term deposit rating is one notch above its Long-Term IDR.

No Government Support Expected: The Government Support Rating (GSR) of 'no support' reflects Fitch's view that senior creditors cannot rely on extraordinary support from the UK authorities in the event the society becomes non-viable, given UK legislation and regulations that provide a framework requiring senior creditors to participate in losses after a failure.

OTHER DEBT AND ISSUER RATINGS: RATING SENSITIVITIES

Nationwide

The SP and SNP debt and deposit ratings and the DCR are primarily sensitive to a change in Nationwide's IDRs. In addition, the SP and DCR would be downgraded to the level of the IDRs and the SNP debt rating one notch below the Long-Term IDR if the society is no longer required to meet its MREL with SNP or more junior instruments only or no longer intends to do so, which Fitch does not expect.

The ratings of the subordinated debt and hybrid securities are primarily sensitive to changes in Nationwide's VR. The ratings of the AT1 securities are also sensitive to a change in Fitch's assessment of the probability of their non-performance relative to the risk captured in the VR.

Fitch does not expect changes to the GSR, given the legislation in place that is likely to require senior creditors to participate in losses for resolving Nationwide.

VMUK and CB

VMUK's and CB's Short-Term IDRs are sensitive to changes in Nationwide's Short-Term IDR. CB's short-term deposit rating is sensitive to changes in its Short-Term IDR.

VMUK's senior unsecured, AT1, and CB's DCR and long-term deposit rating are sensitive to changes in Nationwide's Long-Term IDR.

Fitch does not expect changes to the GSR, given the legislation in place that is likely to require senior creditors to participate in losses for resolving Nationwide.

VR ADJUSTMENTS

The asset quality score of 'a' is below the 'aa' implied category score due to the following adjustment reason: concentrations (negative).

The capitalisation & leverage score of 'a' is below the 'aa' implied category score due to the following adjustment reason: capital flexibility and ordinary support (negative).

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. ESG Considerations

Unless otherwise disclosed in this section, the highest level of ESG credit relevance is a score of '3'. This means ESG issues are credit neutral or have only a minimal credit impact on the entity, either due to their nature or the way in which they are being managed by the entity. Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores are not inputs in the rating process; they are an observation of the materiality and relevance of ESG factors in a rating decision. For more information on Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores, visit www.fitchratings.com/topics/esg/products#esg-relevance-scores.

data.title: Bitcoin Depot Slides as New Regulations Expected to Dent Sales
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Shares of Bitcoin Depot dropped after the cryptocurrency ATM operator said it expects new regulations to weigh on its future sales.

The stock fell 18%, to $2.07, midday Thursday. Shares are still up 27% this year.

Bitcoin Depot said Thursday it expects fourth-quarter revenue to be $112 million to $115 million. Analysts polled by FactSet were projecting $144.8 million.

In several states, recent regulations which impose transaction-size caps or fee caps in several states are likely to put a dent in revenue, Bitcoin Depot said. The Atlanta company has also enhanced its compliance standards.

Executives told analysts on a call that they view the regulatory developments as constructive for the industry's long-term health, despite the financial effects.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: PLATTS: 100--SMR company joins with Indiana to build a first-of-its-kind nuclear energy park
data.description: (Platts)--04Nov2025/506 pm EST/2206 GMT **Firm to help transition state from coal to nuclear **Advanced fuel cycle would make nuclear renewable The small modular reactor firm First American Nuclear plans to build the first US nuclear energy park in Indiana, using a closed-loop fuel cycle to continuously reuse spent nuclear fuel. The company will also relocate its headquarters from Washington state to Indiana and establish manufacturing facilities there, according to a Nov. 4 statement. First American Nuclear will convert existing coal plants to natural gas-fired power facilities, which it can then shift to nuclear power facilities using the same workforce and with minimal changes to equipment. "This provides immediate gas power to meet near-term needs while simultaneously fast-tracking a seamless and cost-effective transition to nuclear," the company said. "Consumers get reliability without higher bills, and the state of Indiana attracts industry with access to an abundant, scalable energy supply." Indiana has largely been a coal-dependent state, but in the past 12 years it has begun a systemic shift toward natural gas. About 5 GW of coal-fired capacity has been retired in the state since 2013, and another 3.9 GW is slated for retirement by the end of 2028, according to the US Energy Information Administration. Natural gas-fired generation has offset most of coal's decline in the state. CLOSING THE LOOP The company said its EAGL-1 small modular reactor would create more fuel than it uses to maintain a reaction, allowing it to eliminate 97% of all highly radioactive nuclear fuel waste. This sets the stage "for nuclear energy to be deemed not only clean, but renewable," the company said. The EAGL-1 design is what is known as a fast breeder reactor. It operates at higher temperatures than conventional water-cooled reactors, enabling it to produce more electricity by creating more fast-moving neutrons. The technology also enables the reactors to make more efficient use of uranium- or thorium-based fuels, which can be bred into plutonium or other types of uranium as the reactor operates. The scaled-down design of the EAGL-1 reactor, which uses lead bismuth as a coolant, allows it to create a closed-fuel cycle where it continuously consumes, produces, and recycles fuel. "Due to the benign chemical properties of lead bismuth, EAGL-1 does not require complex safety systems using exotic materials," the company said. "The result is a compact, simple design with one-third less hardware than other SMRs and the ability to integrate seamlessly with off-the shelf power systems." President Donald Trump in a May executive order made reprocessing and recycling nuclear fuel a national priority. The order instructed the Department of Energy, in cooperation with other agencies, to create a strategy to develop and deploy "advanced fuel cycle capabilities" to create a safe, secure and sustainable long-term fuel cycle. Indiana Republican Governor Mike Braun said in a joint statement with the company that the energy park will create 5,000 jobs "while cementing our state as a leader in clean, reliable nuclear power." The EAGL-1 SMR reactor will be manufactured and assembled in the state using existing factory infrastructure and US supply chains. First American Nuclear will develop a program to attract and retain a nuclear workforce through partnerships with Indiana's technical colleges and universities. "The program will build a pipeline of highly skilled local workers to support the manufacturing and operations of EAGL-1 nuclear components, and the deployment of these systems across the United States and around the world," the company said. — John Siciliano, john.siciliano@spglobal.com-- Edited by Kassia Micek, newsdesk@spglobal.com--Platts Electricity Alert--

data.title: ST LOUIS FED'S MUSALEM Q&A/EVANSVILLE: PEOPLE TEND TO PULL BACK WHEN ENCOUNTER UNCERTAINY; LATELY HAVE HAD SEVERAL LAYERS OF UNCERTAINTY
